title: Listening - Part 4: Restoring Family Photographs
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with Sofia, a photographic restoration specialist. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today I’m talking to Sofia Green, whose job is restoring damaged old photographs digitally. Sofia, how did you begin? || Sofia: My grandmother kept family photos in an old suitcase. One picture of her parents had a large tear across the middle, so I scanned it and tried to repair the missing area on my laptop. [24]When my grandmother recognised details she thought had disappeared for ever, I understood how powerful photographic restoration could be[/24]. Later I studied digital imaging and made it my career. || Presenter: What do clients usually bring you? || Sofia: Everything from faded wedding portraits to pictures damaged in floods. People often assume my first aim is to make every image look clean and modern. In fact, [25]I begin by asking which details and signs of age are meaningful to the owner before altering anything[/25]. A crease or handwritten date may be part of the family's memory. || Presenter: Can software do most of the work automatically now? || Sofia: Some tools remove small marks quickly, which is useful. But they sometimes invent shapes or faces that were never there. [26]I never allow a computer to fill a missing important detail unless I have another photograph or clear evidence to guide me[/26]. Restoration should recover information, not create a more attractive fiction. || Presenter: What project has been particularly demanding? || Sofia: A local sports club gave me hundreds of small photographs to prepare for an anniversary exhibition. Many were unnamed. The repairs were straightforward, but [27]identifying the players required meeting retired members who could recognise faces and uniforms[/27]. Their conversations also produced wonderful stories that were eventually included in the display. || Presenter: Do clients ever request colour in black-and-white images? || Sofia: Frequently. Colour can help younger people connect with a historical picture, but it involves choices. Unless we know the actual colour of a dress or wall, we are guessing. [28]I provide both the original black-and-white restoration and a clearly labelled colour version[/28], so no one confuses an interpretation with historical fact. || Presenter: Do people print restored photos, or just keep digital copies? || Sofia: Both. A digital copy is useful for sharing among relatives, but files can become lost or forgotten. [29]I recommend printing important photographs on good-quality paper as well as saving digital copies in more than one place[/29]. That way a technical failure does not remove a family's visual history again. || Presenter: What gives you the greatest satisfaction? || Sofia: Not perfect pixels on a screen. It is when an image starts a conversation. [30]I love seeing restored photographs encourage family members to tell stories that younger relatives had never heard[/30]. The picture matters because it connects people, not simply because I have made it clearer.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What first showed Sofia the value of photograph restoration?

options.A: Repairing an image for her grandmother.

questions.explanation: Her grandmother recognised details in a torn photo that Sofia restored.

questions.question: What does Sofia do before starting a restoration?

options.A: She decides how to modernise the image.
options.B: She asks owners what should be preserved.

questions.explanation: She discusses meaningful details and signs of age before making alterations.

questions.question: What is Sofia's view of automatic software?

options.A: It should not invent important missing details.
options.B: It is too slow for professional projects.
options.C: It is reliable only for photographs of faces.

questions.explanation: She needs photographic or other clear evidence before filling major missing information.

questions.question: Why was the sports club project demanding?

options.B: The exhibition space was very limited.
options.C: The people in the pictures had to be identified.

questions.explanation: She needed retired members to recognise unnamed players and uniforms.

questions.question: How does Sofia handle colour versions of old photographs?

options.A: She refuses to create them without complete colour information.
options.B: She presents them as interpretations alongside the original style.
options.C: She adds colour only to clothing and buildings.

questions.explanation: She supplies both versions and labels the colour one clearly.

questions.question: How does Sofia advise people to store valuable images?

options.A: Keep printed and multiple digital versions.
options.B: Store original photographs separately from families.

questions.explanation: She recommends good prints and digital copies saved in more than one place.

questions.question: What does Sofia most enjoy about her work?

options.B: Making badly damaged photographs appear perfect.
options.C: Helping photographs lead to family conversations.

questions.explanation: She values restored images prompting stories for younger relatives.
